source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide
author: Robert Cribb, Dean Jobb, David McKie, Fred Vallance-Jones

extracts.extract: In order to get away from the numbers, you first need to get close to them.
extracts.chapter: Following the money: business angle in investigations
extracts.notes: Paradox attributed to David Stewart-Patterson: must understand numbers to tell human stories

extracts.extract: I never read the press release until I've read the company's financial filings. This prevents me from being diverted by the company's public relations department.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters
extracts.notes: Attributed to Jay Taparia. Read filings before press releases to avoid spin.

extracts.extract: When reviewing key figures in a financial statement, it's important to read the MD&A for reasons explained earlier in this chapter. It's also important to notice the notes that may be attached to the numbers. The notes indicate the section of the report that provides important context. The language in the notes and MD&A tends to be technical and jargon-heavy.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters
extracts.notes: Applied to BlackBerry case study

extracts.extract: Once you have a basic understanding, it's a good idea to run the numbers by an expert who has no vested interest, such as a business professor who studies the company, to help deepen your understanding and perspective.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters

extracts.extract: Financial statements can be seen as tip sheets to be mined for story ideas.
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters

extracts.extract: Private businesses are more secretive about their financial performance because, under normal circumstances, they are not obliged to disclose information to the public. The key is finding documents—in addition to insolvency records, the courts, and bankruptcy trustees and receivers—that they don't want you to see.

The Notice of Intention is the beginning of a process that could unfold with the drama that is usually contained in many instances where companies face the prospect of going out of business. Journalists wanting to get the real story should follow the paper trail, which can begin with the OSB, then wind its way to the court file containing more material related to the case, and eventually to interviews with the major players in the drama.
extracts.notes: Office of the Superintendent of Bankruptcy as starting point

extracts.extract: Ninety-nine per cent of business is done behind closed doors. This is your one chance to pull back the veil and see what's going on.
extracts.notes: David Debenham on journalists missing real story by not reading court documents in insolvency cases

extracts.extract: With CanLII, you see the sausage after it has been made. But some of the interesting stuff is the evidence being made in the sausage. The documents on CanLII are basically saying, at the end of the day, this is what the judge decided. Well, that's interesting, but what evidence was he looking at?
extracts.notes: Debenham. Rulings are summaries; the underlying court file has the real story.

extracts.extract: The owner or president of a private company may not want to divulge the degree to which her company is enriching a bank account (legally or illegally), but she may have no choice in a bankruptcy, divorce hearing, or lawsuit. In court, people have to disclose their money.
extracts.notes: Courts as involuntary disclosure mechanism for private companies

extracts.extract: If creditors are constantly owed money, or if payments are continually behind schedule, then that would tell you about the company's ability to make ends meet. And this is why it's helpful to have lists of creditors.

Government departments and agencies that regulate businesses can also be wonderful sources of information. You won't necessarily find out how much money the company is making or losing, but you will discover a thing or two about its products and track record as a corporate citizen, whether it was fined for a particular transgression, or at least inspected.

In addition to determining whether a company is private or public, it's important for you to find out how a particular business is regulated. If a private company makes prescription drugs, for example, then its activities are monitored by Health Canada and the US Food and Drug Administration.
extracts.notes: First step: identify which regulator monitors the company

extracts.extract: When investigating companies that do business in Canada and the United States, it's useful to check with the regulatory authorities and investigative bodies in both countries.
When investigating companies, one of the first questions to ask is whether the workforce, or at least part of it, is unionized.

Some unions use access-to-information and freedom-of-information laws to extract even more data. The union might be concerned about the company's increasing tendency to cut corners and save money by flouting certain safety regulations. To get documented proof of such activities, the union might have pursued inspection reports or correspondence within Transport Canada, the CFIA, or the provincial agriculture ministry.
extracts.notes: Unions as pre-existing FOI requesters who may already hold documents

extracts.extract: A union may not be interested in painting its employer in too positive a light, especially during contract negotiations, so use its information with that in mind.

Keep track of dates when public companies must file their reports with the securities regulators. As soon as the reports are filed, scour them for news, such as lawsuits, lost earnings, or surprising numbers you won't find in news releases. Develop a good rapport with an expert who can help you compare the information contained in the numbers to what the company is saying publicly. The larger the disconnect, the better the story.
extracts.notes: Disconnect between filings and public statements = story

extracts.extract: Get your own information from the regulators, consumers, courts, competitors, and creditors, particularly from people who are angry with the company and therefore won't mind sharing their information, which still needs to be verified.

For the investigative journalist, access requests are a basic research tool. The legislation opens the door for in-depth research into the machinations of government, the reasons why decisions are made, and their impact on the public. If used strategically, information laws can help discover how taxpayer dollars are spent, warn us of public health risks, uncover corruption, and hold elected officials to account.
extracts.chapter: Getting Behind Closed Doors: Using the Information Laws

extracts.extract: Usually, ATI and FOI requests are not useful for reporters working on tight deadlines. It's not generally a tool for breaking news stories. It is a method for pursuing issues that are likely to be as important in a month or two as they are now, stories that go beyond the press release or official comment of the day. And it's a way of pursuing issues that aren't part of the public debate at all, but should be.
extracts.chapter: Getting Behind Closed Doors: Using the Information Laws
extracts.notes: FOI is for slow-burn stories not breaking news

extracts.extract: The first step in obtaining government records is not filing an information request. In fact, filing a formal written request should only happen after exhausting all informal methods of obtaining the records. Simply asking for them is the best initial approach.
extracts.notes: Ask informally first; formal FOI is last resort

extracts.extract: Another important step before filing a formal request is seeing what information has already been released. Completed requests are a matter of public record, and the records themselves are easily accessible, usually through an informal request.
extracts.notes: Check completed FOI requests before filing new ones

extracts.extract: The more informed and detailed your question, the more complete and precise the response will have to be. Filing a well-crafted information request can take a lot of work. You may need details of a government department's record-keeping and managerial processes, or a company's subsidiaries and regulatory background. The more you know about the question, the better your chances of plucking out the most relevant and important records from government files.
extracts.notes: Specificity of request correlates with quality of response

extracts.extract: There are effectively two kinds of ATI requests. Fishing expedition requests are broader in scope and use open-ended language. The surgical approach comes when you know the specific question you want answered, or, even better, the specific documents or records that contain those answers. The more specific the question, the more specific the response must be.
extracts.notes: Two strategies: broad fishing vs. surgical precision

extracts.extract: One of the best ways to do this is to speak to insiders at the particular government department who are willing to help you understand the issues, what records are going to raise alarm bells, and who can give you advice on how to address these. Such people are a great resource for helping to craft a specific request. Their knowledge of a department's paper trail can be immensely valuable.
extracts.notes: Cultivate department insiders to guide FOI wording

extracts.extract: When requesting databases, you should attempt to find out as much as possible about the data, how it's stored, and what fields it contains. From there, you can determine which fields are likely to be protected by legislation and which should be public. That lets you craft a far more specific request, which is likely to succeed. Doing such work up front saves time later.
If the records you are requesting are likely to contain personal information that would be covered by privacy exemptions, then you should specify that you are waiving any demand for that material.
extracts.notes: Proactively waive personal info to speed up processing

extracts.extract: Dividing large requests into several smaller requests helps maximize the bonus search time you receive.
extracts.notes: Each request gets free initial search time; splitting multiplies free hours

extracts.extract: Time and space are two common parameters for limiting requests. If you've asked for 10 years' worth of records, for example, cutting that time frame in half could quickly reduce response time and cost. Likewise, rather than asking for records for the entire province or region, limiting the request to a particular city or rural area should reduce the number of offices involved.

Be careful when you compromise on aspects of the request. If you're looking at how a particular government program has served the public, cutting down a request to six months' worth of records instead of a year's worth might make your resulting analysis vulnerable to criticism for failing to account for special circumstances in that limited period of time.
extracts.notes: Narrowing requests can undermine analytical completeness

extracts.extract: Make sure you understand the limitations you will face when you begin writing. Don't represent your research material as exhaustive when it is not. The records may still form the basis of an important story, but you should acknowledge any shortcomings in your evidence.

Filing for access to information records is often a frustrating waste of time in investigative stories because the requests are seldom completed in time for your deadlines and frequently the material is so heavily redacted as to be useless. Still, it always pays to fire off some requests at the start of your probe.
extracts.notes: Attributed to Julian Sher. File FOI early even if odds are low.

extracts.extract: People—their misdeeds and heroics and struggles—make your story. But documents and the facts they contain can provide much of the scaffolding to build that tale.
extracts.notes: Julian Sher. Documents = scaffolding; people = story.

extracts.extract: In baseball terms, if you get a hit in one out of every three at-bats, you're doing well. The same goes for access. One request might yield a story, another some interesting background material and the third could be a strikeout. I think the best requests flow from intuition. They could come from reading a news item, noting something said by a cabinet minister or following up on a hunch.
extracts.notes: Jim Bronskill. ~33% success rate for FOI requests is normal.

extracts.extract: The media generally make a one-day wonder out of auditor's reports, but they often trigger a flurry of ongoing work behind government scenes. I submitted various requests to a number of federal departments.
extracts.chapter: Memos, briefing notes, reports, and studies
extracts.notes: Bronskill. Auditor reports seed FOI requests for government follow-up records.

extracts.extract: It's important for journalists to see the documents in their entirety and not rely on a selective interpretation. The research material that politicians rely on to make their case can be more textured or even contradictory than they might portray in front of the media.
extracts.chapter: Memos, briefing notes, reports, and studies

extracts.extract: Rather than filing one request for all of the emails, Press says he broke them into five-day time frames for the emails beginning in the days just after the information breach until the official announcement came. This helped cut down the response time and eliminated many of the fees he was originally asked to pay.
extracts.notes: Jordan Press technique for data breach emails at HRSDC

extracts.extract: Press then took a further step with a request for records triggered by his own previous requests for insights into how officials were reacting to the published revelations behind closed government doors. This is a valuable trick often used by seasoned information requesters to track fallout from published stories based on the original records and the political messaging that follows.
extracts.notes: File FOI for government reaction to your own earlier FOI stories

extracts.extract: The Canadian access-to-information request would likely have been useless in this case. There is a two-year delay at the Canadian Foreign Affairs ATI branch, and exemptions are usually far more heavily applied in Canada to withhold records.
extracts.notes: Stanley Tromp. When Canadian ATI is slow, try US FOIA for same bilateral records.

extracts.extract: Usually, an impartial analysis of the findings will be far more textured than typically reassuring government messaging. Testing and data are clinical. But they typically point to a strong human story.

Ideas for these kinds of stories are all around us. Often, such testing is mentioned in news stories that refer to results contained in government press releases. The opportunity for digging deeper lies in obtaining the primary records and conducting first-hand analysis of what they say, talking to experts, and finding the people who are impacted.
extracts.notes: Go past the press release to the underlying data

extracts.extract: Working with inspection records often requires a three-step process: understand how the inspection system works, request the records, then analyze them.

Perhaps the most basic question to ask any public official responsible for protecting the public interest is simply this: When something goes wrong, where do you write it down? Answers to that question will often provide a starting point that will help you understand where to go next.
extracts.notes: Foundational question for any regulatory beat

extracts.extract: How often do officials conduct their inspections? Are there mandatory time frames? Do they call the company ahead of time or make surprise visits? What exactly do they look for? Where do they record what they see? What are the names of those records? What kind of information is in them? When inspectors get back to the office after one of these inspections, what do they do with their findings? Does the information go into a database? If so, what's the database called? Is it public?
extracts.notes: Full checklist of questions to understand an inspection regime

extracts.extract: Because they might contain private information, the idea is to ask for a blank copy of the inspection form or the fields contained in any resulting database. Blank forms will let you see how the department or ministry catalogues its inspection information.
extracts.notes: Request blank forms as a non-sensitive starting point

extracts.extract: Cumulative data might prompt further questions. If you access data that shows problems in several water-testing plants, it would be in the public interest to identify which ones in particular. For this level of detail, where you'd likely be seeking information about private companies or individuals, you'll most certainly have to file formal information requests.
extracts.notes: Aggregate data -> anomalies -> targeted FOI for specifics

extracts.extract: Inspection records, such as those involving unlicensed home daycares, offer a rare glimpse into the appalling conditions some children endure. Publicizing these reports helps to show why change is needed. And public support for change is often the first step in changing government policy.
extracts.notes: Laurie Monsebraaten on the policy impact of publishing inspection records

extracts.extract: While doing preliminary research before an FOI request is always worthwhile, it's essential for FOI requests for data. You need to know how the data you're requesting is gathered and stored in order to ask for it effectively.
extracts.notes: Chad Skelton

extracts.extract: Many government departments release records as PDF files that are difficult to analyze or get into spreadsheet formats. To save time and annoyance, you'll want to do your due diligence on the data, how they're structured, the format, and the fields of information inside. It will help you ask for the records in a specific format and pre-emptively avoid fields with clearly sensitive private information that will hold up the process.
extracts.notes: Specify format in request to avoid unusable PDFs

extracts.extract: When government pushback comes, Skelton advises speaking with the data experts inside the ministry or department to try and clear it directly rather than working through a communications mediator. Public relations folks and FOI coordinators are often not technically literate enough to know how data is maintained and how it can be released.
extracts.notes: Chad Skelton. Talk to data people, not comms people.

extracts.extract: When Skelton filed requests for paper records earlier in his career, the success rate was about one story for every 10 requests. With data requests, the success ratio is the reverse: about 90 per cent. That's because when you finally get the data you're asking for—say salary files, crime data, parking ticket data—it's almost always worth at least a story.
extracts.notes: Chad Skelton. Data FOIs have ~90% story yield vs ~10% for paper.

extracts.extract: Data journalism is made up of three disciplines that are distinct yet interdependent. The first is the traditional skills of computer-assisted reporting, which use data analysis and mapping software to identify newsworthy patterns and trends in data sets. The second discipline is data visualization. The third leg of data journalism is custom Web development, using Internet coding skills to create unique interactive news applications.
extracts.notes: Framework: analysis + visualization + interactive development

extracts.extract: Generally speaking, the more that a database records something that directly affects people, the more likely it is to be the basis for a compelling investigation. The closer the data gets to people's lives, the more impact your story is likely to have.
extracts.chapter: What Makes a Good Data Investigation?

extracts.extract: Don't make the mistake of believing that just because something is recorded in a database, you can miraculously make a story appear from it. Analyzing boring data will produce a boring story.
extracts.chapter: What Makes a Good Data Investigation?

extracts.extract: Almost any time a government agency administers, tracks, or inspects something, the results will be collected in such a system. Think of the number of times you have been to a government office and have filled out a form. The information from those forms almost certainly ends up in a database somewhere.
extracts.chapter: Steps in the Development of a Database Investigation
extracts.notes: If there is a form, there is a database

extracts.extract: The first thing you need to do is come to understand what is in the database. This means opening the tables, seeing what fields are present, and what kind of data each field holds. You also need to figure out what any codes in the database mean, and see if any dirty data is present. Dirty data includes misspellings of names, fields that are empty when they should be filled, and mistakes.
extracts.chapter: Steps in the Development of a Database Investigation

extracts.extract: The next step is to make a list of queries you would like to run. From there, create and save your queries, keeping track of what each one is for, and developing a list of questions, based on the data, that you will take to human sources. Interviews, further paper-based research, and rigorous accuracy checks of your results will follow.
extracts.chapter: Steps in the Development of a Database Investigation
extracts.notes: Data analysis -> questions -> interviews -> verification

extracts.extract: You will need to look for examples on the ground to illustrate trends that have appeared in your data. If you can't find your story in the real world, you should question whether the story is real at all.
extracts.chapter: Steps in the Development of a Database Investigation
extracts.notes: If data patterns have no real-world examples, question the data

extracts.extract: The first thing you need to do is find out as much as you can about the data and the real-world situation that is being tracked. Look up secondary sources, talk with people who are familiar with the issue or the information. Obtain any public annual reports or summaries produced from the data. Don't forget to look in the official access guides such as Info Source, the federal directory of records published by the Treasury Board.

You may also want to cultivate a source in the information technology or data systems branch of the agency. These people know the data far better than their superiors do, and usually know far more than the media-relations people.
extracts.notes: IT staff know the data better than managers or PR

extracts.extract: The request checklist: Describe the database as specifically as possible, using the information you gathered beforehand. Ask that it be provided in an electronic format you can work with, such as Excel, JSON or delimited text. Try to avoid having data provided in PDF format, as this makes it difficult to extract the data for analysis. If you don't have them already, ask for full details on the record layout, all codes used in the database, and the file format.
extracts.chapter: Using freedom-of-information requests to obtain data
extracts.notes: Three-point checklist for data FOI requests

extracts.extract: The bottom line here is that database systems are designed to make data extraction easy. Programming should almost never be required, and if it is, it shouldn't have to be extensive or expensive.
extracts.chapter: Using freedom-of-information requests to obtain data
extracts.notes: Challenge inflated cost estimates for data extraction

extracts.extract: Often, agencies simply don't want to hand over electronic information because they know what you could do with it. They want to control the data. Or they're just nervous because no one ever asked before. They may also ask what you want the data or what story you intend to write. These are questions you don't have to answer, though providing general answers can help build trust.
extracts.chapter: Using freedom-of-information requests to obtain data

extracts.extract: If the information is critical and you can't negotiate a compromise, consider their argument and appeal if necessary. You can also agree on what parts of the database can be released, and then complain about the ones that are subject to disagreement. Getting some of the data allows you to do a potential story, which upon publication, may make it easier to obtain more data if you show your reporting is fair and accurate.
extracts.chapter: Using freedom-of-information requests to obtain data
extracts.notes: Publish with partial data to build trust for getting more

extracts.extract: One advantage of custom building a database is that the journalist will have an intimate understanding of the data by the time the work is completed. This makes it easier to decide how to query the data. In fact, when a database is custom built, the data included is often exactly what the journalists realized they need. The data structure is designed with specific querying strategies in mind.
extracts.notes: Building your own DB from documents produces deeper understanding

extracts.extract: One of the dangers of data journalism is that wrong results look as good as right ones. The old expression garbage in, garbage out is especially applicable to data work. Scan individual fields to look for unusually large or unusually small entries—outliers. These may be mistakes that could add up to bigger mistakes. Or they could be really great stories. Verify these outliers using external sources, such as printed reports, or people familiar with the data.

Look for fields with no data in them. If data has been collected inconsistently, any summary results will be questionable, and you shouldn't publish, broadcast, or post anything based on the results until you are certain the omissions won't render your conclusions invalid.

Go back and check your original data, as well as your queries and spreadsheet formulas, to make sure you haven't made mistakes. Ask a colleague or someone in the accounting or finance department to double-check your numbers. Be especially careful if a pattern you found in the data cannot be verified in the real world.

Don't hesitate to present your main results to the authorities who control the data. You will want to do this in any case, in order to get their comment on the record. If officials protest that your results are wrong, take heed.
extracts.notes: Present findings to data owners for comment before publication

extracts.extract: Sometimes it's not the big picture that counts, but the fine details. While large data investigations often involve extensive analysis, just scrolling through a data set can give you story ideas. Databases kept on your hard drive and updated from time to time can provide you with quick reference material for day-to-day stories.
extracts.chapter: Databases as reference sources and tip sheets
extracts.notes: Keep and update databases as standing reference for daily beat work

extracts.extract: No amount of data analysis will produce a story on its own. To get the story you need to combine data with traditional techniques. Sometimes, computer analysis will provide rock-solid, irrefutable facts that can themselves be highlighted in a story. Just as often, analysis will provide you with good questions to ask.
extracts.chapter: Combining Data Journalism with Traditional Reporting
extracts.notes: Data provides facts or questions; human reporting lands the story

extracts.extract: The data can point you to the story, but only reporting will land it. This is why it is called computer-assisted reporting and not reporter-assisted computing.
extracts.chapter: Combining Data Journalism with Traditional Reporting

extracts.extract: A list of documents and interviews: whether kept electronically or on paper, this should detail the author of each document, where and how it was created, the essential points it contains, and why you found the document useful. A similar approach works in organizing interview transcripts.
extracts.chapter: Preparing to Write, Right From the Start

extracts.extract: A chronology keeps track of every date that came up in your investigation, including the dates mentioned by sources during interviews, the dates of meetings, the dates on the documents you have obtained, and when important scholarly papers were published. The sequence of events exposes inconsistencies in explanations and contradictions between what people say they knew and what they must have known.
extracts.chapter: Preparing to Write, Right From the Start
extracts.notes: Chronology as analytical tool that also serves as writing scaffold

extracts.extract: Using Excel for your chronology is an excellent option, in large part because dates and times can be entered randomly, and then sorted chronologically. Each field in the record or row of the Excel table can contain a date, contact person, a short narrative explaining the importance of each entry, or a hyperlink to the website, PDF, or any other source.
extracts.chapter: Preparing to Write, Right From the Start

extracts.extract: Once you feel you are ready to begin to write, tell a colleague or friend in a few sentences what the story is about. Reducing the story to a short statement helps you to organize the major facts and important points in a logical manner. Complex events and technical jargon must be put into everyday language. Explaining things aloud forces you to get to the heart of the story and why it is important.

Make sure you emphasize the information that is most relevant to your audience, not the information that was hardest to obtain. The public interest, and not behind-the-scenes journalistic challenges, should always be the defining consideration in choosing where to focus an investigative story.
extracts.notes: Resist effort bias: hard-won info is not necessarily most important

extracts.extract: The information you collect through documents and data should hold true in the real world. If it does not, is it because the data is incomplete or improperly gathered? Could you have collected records that prove one side of an issue and missed documents that reveal contrary evidence? You must test your conclusions and carefully consider any discrepancies between what you have researched and what you see and hear.
extracts.notes: If documents and reality don't match, re-examine before writing

extracts.extract: Think of the facts and figures you have collected as the journey rather than the destination. A database may tell you where to look. It may take you to the particular city, school, person, business, or accident site. It may tell you how that specific incident relates to all other similar incidents. But it does not tell a story. You must take the extra step of going to the city, school, person, business, or accident site to find the scenes, voices, and real-life texture that will make the numbers come to life.
extracts.notes: Data = journey, not destination; go to the scene

extracts.extract: A number tossed into a story without context has virtually no value. Is a 20 per cent increase in pollution emissions significant or not? It is not enough to tell your audience that city hall spends  million a year defending lawsuits against police officers; you need to explain how this compares to the amount spent over the last three or four years, or to cities of a similar size.
extracts.notes: Numbers need temporal, geographic, or categorical context

extracts.extract: Stories that reduce complicated issues to good-versus-evil scenarios risk losing their sense of authenticity. Always present the grey areas in your story with the knowledge that its complexities only enrich the authenticity of what you write. A person, company, or government department portrayed as a one-dimensional villain is less interesting than a fully realized, complex subject.

Show, don't tell is an often-repeated rule of narrative writing—and for good reason. Factual and statistical information must be in the story, as a function of your role as a researcher; but as a storyteller, your job is to appeal to the heart. You need to combine the simple declarative statements with strong, visual examples.
Who becomes Character. What becomes Action (what happened). Where becomes Setting. When becomes Chronology. Why becomes Motivation or Causality. How becomes Process (how it happened).
extracts.notes: Roy Peter Clark's transformation of 5 Ws into narrative elements

extracts.extract: Some audits may appear to be available on the corporation websites, but these are often mere summaries that leave out details about spending habits. As a reporter, you must never be satisfied with the summarized versions. The details are the key to your story, so always ask for as many as possible.

For journalists, these authorities should be covered with the same diligence as any government department. For example, airport authorities are obliged to file reports about steps they're taking to secure a safe environment for planes to take off and land. Finding out when these reports are filed and obtaining them—usually through an access-to-information request—can reveal details about issues the authority may have.
extracts.notes: Quasi-public entities have filing obligations that create obtainable records

extracts.extract: It's also important to know which legislative committees, be they at the federal or provincial level, are responsible for monitoring the drug industry and other sectors of the economy. You can use the Hansard searches described in Chapter 4 to find out more.

It becomes useful to examine the pieces of legislation that committees review, the witnesses they call before them, and so on. And, because many of the businesses we cover tend to operate in the United States—drug companies and carmakers being among them—it's useful to peruse the legislative committees in that country.
extracts.notes: US congressional committee research may already exist on your topic

extracts.extract: Check the analyst's potential bias. If the analyst in question has only positive things to say about a company, make sure that the person doesn't have a financial stake in how well the company performs. Any conflict of interest should be stated prominently.
extracts.chapter: Getting Information from Outside the Company

extracts.extract: Government departments are increasingly sophisticated in identifying politically sensitive requests, and most major departments will have procedures for dealing with communications implications of sensitive requests. If you're making a politically sensitive request, your request will be delayed. You should also assume that communications advisors in government are looking at your request and developing communications products to help the department deal with the fallout from your request.
extracts.notes: Alasdair Roberts. Expect politically sensitive requests to be flagged and delayed.

extracts.extract: Individuals who want to know how their request was handled behind the scenes can file separate requests for the material. The records should show when the request was received, the office that held the records, whether there were consultations with communications staff and ministerial advisors, and the back-and-forth communications between officials on the process of handling the request.
extracts.notes: File FOI about the handling of your own FOI request

extracts.extract: The federal government has tried to undercut the ATI law by creating new organizations to provide public service but failing to include those new organizations under the law. More than 100 public institutions sit beyond the reach of public access.
extracts.notes: Alasdair Roberts. Privatization creates FOI blind spots.

extracts.extract: Investigative reporters must ensure the information they report is accurate and complete. Allegations must be based on verifiable facts and come from sources that are reliable and credible. Their stories must be fair and strike a balance between differing points of view. And they must make every effort to offer those accused of wrongdoing a chance to refute the allegations against them.

Journalists should view the law of defamation not as a barrier to reporting but as a guarantee of quality journalism. If reporters were free to report whatever they liked about whomever they liked, any notion of media credibility would vanish.
extracts.notes: Libel law as quality control, not obstacle

extracts.extract: Innuendo—the message that can be read between the lines of a news report—may also be defamatory. Suppose a journalist reports that a politician lives an expensive lifestyle, makes only a modest salary, and supports proposals put forward by a businessperson who is also a long-time friend. Each one of these facts, in isolation, may be true. But when tied together, they convey the defamatory impression that the politician is taking bribes or kickbacks.
extracts.notes: True facts can create defamatory innuendo through juxtaposition

extracts.extract: Journalists cannot duck a libel action by claiming they have only repeated or quoted what someone else has said. While a source makes a defamatory statement to a single person—the journalist—it is the news organization that disseminates it to a wide audience, inflicting the real damage to reputation.
extracts.notes: Republication doctrine: quoting a defamatory source does not shield you

extracts.extract: It is pointless to begin writing until you are locked and loaded on your main theme and where you are going to take it. Trying to write a complex story without a strong command of your material, ideas, and approach is a frustrating and futile exercise. So be willing to recognize fundamental flaws that present themselves at this stage. It is better to return to the research now than to surge ahead regardless.
extracts.notes: If the thesis has holes, go back to research, don't start writing

extracts.extract: Entering the figures into a spreadsheet will help you learn the numbers. Not only will you start to understand the business, you will also start to see trends (and maybe even some parts of the story).
extracts.chapter: Financial filings and corporate disclosure for reporters
extracts.notes: Jay Taparia. Data entry as learning method.

extracts.extract: She went through every page of the documents and noted in an Excel sheet the date, severity, location, and details of every incident or inspection that was seen as presenting a danger to the public. I left off the low-severity ones, simply because there were way too many.
extracts.notes: Leslie Young on Gardiner Expressway. Manual extraction from 2000+ pages into structured Excel.

extracts.extract: Use numbers sparingly—just enough to get your point across. Viewers and listeners who want more information can be directed to additional information posted on the broadcaster's website.
extracts.notes: David Stewart-Patterson. Broadcast: minimize numbers; web: provide detail.
